description: Practice present tense endings for regular and irregular verbs.

questions.question: Ik ___ (hebben) een appel.
questions.explanation: For 'ik', use the verb stem. The stem of 'hebben' is 'heb'.

questions.question: Jij ___ (lopen) naar huis.
questions.explanation: For 'jij/je', add -t to the stem: loop + t = loopt.

questions.question: Wij ___ (werken) vandaag.
questions.explanation: For plural subjects (wij, jullie, zij), use the infinitive form.

questions.question: Hij ___ (kunnen) goed zwemmen.
questions.explanation: Modal verbs have irregular forms. 'Hij kan' is correct.

questions.question: What is the stem of 'reizen' (to travel)?
questions.explanation: Remove -en, then change z→s at the end of a stem: reizen → reiz → reis.

questions.question: Hij ___ (zijn) ziek vandaag.
questions.explanation: 'Hij/zij/het' always uses 'is' with the verb zijn.

questions.question: Jullie ___ (zijn) moe.
questions.explanation: For plural subjects, 'zijn' stays as 'zijn'.

questions.question: Ik ___ (willen) koffie.
questions.explanation: Modal verb 'willen' → ik wil (stem, no -t).

questions.question: Zij (she) ___ (werken) bij een bank.
questions.explanation: For 'zij' (singular), add -t to the stem: werk + t = werkt.

questions.question: What happens to the -t ending when 'jij' comes AFTER the verb?
questions.explanation: The -t drops with inversion: 'Werkt jij?' → 'Werk jij?' (t-drop rule).

description: Test your intuition on Dutch noun genders.

questions.question: Which article: 'meisje' (girl)?
questions.explanation: Diminutives ending in -je always take 'het'.

questions.question: Which article: 'auto' (car)?
questions.explanation: 'Auto' is a de-word.

questions.question: Which article: 'boeken' (books — plural)?
questions.explanation: All Dutch plural nouns take 'de'.

questions.question: Which article: 'vrijheid' (freedom)?
questions.explanation: All nouns ending in -heid take 'de'.

questions.question: Which article: 'water'?
questions.explanation: 'Water' is a neuter noun: het water.

questions.question: Which article: 'kind' (child)?
questions.explanation: 'Het kind' — this is a common irregular het-word to memorise.

questions.question: Which article: 'huis' (house)?
questions.explanation: 'Het huis' — neuter noun.

questions.question: Which article: 'tafel' (table)?
questions.explanation: 'De tafel' — common object nouns are often de-words.

questions.question: All nouns ending in -ing are…
questions.explanation: Words ending in -ing (like 'opleiding', 'vergadering') always take 'de'.

questions.question: All diminutives (words ending in -je) take which article?
questions.explanation: This is one of the most reliable rules: -je words are ALWAYS het-words.

description: Master the position of verbs in Dutch sentences.

questions.question: In a standard Dutch main clause, the conjugated verb is always in position…
questions.explanation: This is the V2 (verb-second) rule: the finite verb is always element #2.

questions.question: Which sentence is correct?
questions.explanation: When a time word starts the sentence, inversion occurs: verb stays 2nd, subject follows.

questions.question: Where does 'niet' go in: 'Ik begrijp het ___.'?
questions.explanation: 'Niet' usually comes at the end when negating the whole verb action.

questions.question: In a subordinate clause (starting with 'omdat'), where does the verb go?
questions.explanation: In subordinate clauses, the verb moves to the END: 'omdat ik ziek ben'.

questions.question: Which is the correct subordinate clause?
questions.explanation: In subclauses, the verb goes last: 'omdat ik ziek ben'.

questions.question: Infinitives and participles in a verb cluster go to the…
questions.explanation: In Dutch, infinitives and past participles cluster at the END of the sentence.

questions.question: What is the correct word order? '___ ga ik naar school.'
questions.explanation: A time adverb like 'Morgen' can start a sentence, causing inversion.

description: Subject, object, and possessive pronouns in Dutch.

questions.question: What is the object form of 'jij'?
questions.explanation: The object form of 'jij' is 'jou' (stressed) or 'je' (unstressed).

questions.question: What is the possessive form of 'wij'?
questions.explanation: 'Ons' (before het-words) and 'onze' (before de-words/plurals) are the possessives for 'wij'.

questions.question: Which is the formal/polite 'you' in Dutch?
questions.explanation: 'U' is the formal second-person pronoun, used with elders or strangers.

questions.question: What is the object form of 'hij'?
questions.explanation: Object form of 'hij' is 'hem': 'Ik zie hem.' (I see him.)

questions.question: Which pronoun means 'them' as a direct object?
questions.explanation: 'Hen' is the direct object form ('Ik zie hen'). 'Hun' is used as indirect object.

questions.question: What is the possessive for 'jij' (informal you)?
questions.explanation: 'Jouw' (stressed) or 'je' (unstressed) are the possessive forms of 'jij'.

questions.question: Fill in: '___ heten Jan en Marie.' (They are named Jan and Marie.)
questions.explanation: 'Zij' or 'Ze' are the subject forms (they). 'Hun/Hen' are object forms.

questions.question: Which word negates a noun without an article? 'Ik heb ___ tijd.'
questions.explanation: 'Geen' negates nouns (without or with 'een'): 'Ik heb geen tijd.'

questions.question: Which word negates a verb? 'Ik slaap ___ goed.'
questions.explanation: 'Niet' negates verbs and adjectives: 'Ik slaap niet goed.'

questions.question: Correct sentence: 'I don't have a car.'
questions.explanation: 'Geen' replaces the indefinite article + noun: 'Ik heb geen auto.'

questions.question: Where does 'niet' go with a prepositional phrase? 'Ik ga ___ naar school.'
questions.explanation: 'Niet' goes BEFORE the prepositional phrase it negates.

questions.question: Which is correct: 'That is not my bag.'
questions.explanation: When negating a possessive, 'niet' goes before it: 'niet mijn tas'.

questions.question: Which is correct to negate 'De hond is groot'?
questions.explanation: 'Niet' negates adjectives and comes right before them.

description: Form the simple past with zijn, hebben, and regular verbs.

questions.question: What is the simple past (preterite) of 'zijn' for 'ik'?
questions.explanation: 'Ik was' is the past tense of 'zijn'. Completely irregular.

questions.question: What is the past tense of 'hebben' for 'jij'?
questions.explanation: 'Jij had' is the past tense of 'hebben'.

questions.question: For a regular verb with a 't kofschip stem, the past tense ending is…
questions.explanation: If the last letter of the stem is in 't kofschip (t, k, f, s, ch, p), you add -te/-ten.

questions.question: Past tense of 'werken' (to work) for 'ik'?
questions.explanation: Stem is 'werk'. 'k' is in 't kofschip → add -te: 'ik werkte'.

questions.question: Past tense of 'leven' (to live) for 'ik'?
questions.explanation: Stem is 'leef' (v→f). 'f' is in 't kofschip → BUT v in stem means -de: 'ik leefde'.

questions.question: Which auxiliary verb ('zijn' or 'hebben') is used with 'gaan' (to go) in the perfect tense?
questions.explanation: Motion/change-of-state verbs use 'zijn': 'Ik ben gegaan'.

questions.question: Perfect tense: 'I have eaten.' = ?
questions.explanation: 'Eten' uses 'hebben'. Past participle is 'gegeten': 'Ik heb gegeten.'

description: When to add -e to adjectives in Dutch.

questions.question: Which is correct: 'a big house' (het huis)?
questions.explanation: After 'een' before a het-word, the adjective does NOT get -e: 'een groot huis'.

questions.question: Which is correct: 'the big house'?
questions.explanation: After 'het' (definite), the adjective DOES get -e: 'het grote huis'.

questions.question: Which is correct: 'a red car' (de auto)?
questions.explanation: Before a de-word with 'een', the adjective always gets -e: 'een rode auto'.

questions.question: Which is correct: 'beautiful flowers' (de bloemen — plural)?
questions.explanation: In plural, adjectives always get -e: 'mooie bloemen'.

questions.question: Adjectives that are used alone after a verb (predicatively) get -e ending?
questions.explanation: Predicative adjectives (after zijn, worden) NEVER get -e: 'De auto is rood.'

questions.question: Which is correct: 'an old woman'? (de vrouw)
questions.explanation: Before a de-word with 'een', always add -e: 'een oude vrouw'.

description: Kunnen, mogen, moeten, willen, zullen, hoeven.

questions.question: Which modal expresses 'must / have to'?
questions.explanation: 'Moeten' means must/have to: 'Ik moet studeren.'

questions.question: Which modal expresses 'may / to be allowed to'?
questions.explanation: 'Mogen' means may/be allowed: 'Mag ik hier zitten?'

questions.question: Which modal expresses 'can / to be able to'?
questions.explanation: 'Kunnen' means can/be able to: 'Ik kan zwemmen.'

questions.question: Correct sentence: 'I don't have to go.'
questions.explanation: 'Hoeven … niet te' expresses 'don't have to'. 'Moet niet' means 'must not'.

questions.question: Where does the infinitive go in a modal sentence: 'Hij wil ...'?
options: After the modal at the end
questions.explanation: The dependent infinitive always comes at the END: 'Hij wil koffie drinken.'

questions.question: Which is correct: 'You are not allowed to smoke here.'
options: Je hoeft hier niet te roken.
questions.explanation: 'Mogen' expresses permission. 'Mag niet' = not allowed.

description: How separable verbs work in main and subordinate clauses.

questions.question: What happens to a separable verb in a main clause? E.g. 'opbellen' (to call)
questions.explanation: The prefix separates and moves to the END: 'Ik bel hem op.'

questions.question: Correct sentence with 'meenemen' (to take along): 'I take the book along.'
questions.explanation: Prefix 'mee' goes to end: 'Ik neem het boek mee.'

questions.question: In a subordinate clause, does a separable verb split up?
questions.explanation: In subclauses the verb goes to the end as one unit: '...omdat hij het boek meeneemt.'

questions.question: What is the past participle of 'opbellen'?
questions.explanation: The ge- is inserted between the prefix and stem: op + ge + beld = opgebeld.

questions.question: Which sentence correctly uses 'uitgaan' (to go out)?
questions.explanation: Prefix 'uit' separates to the end. With inversion: 'Vanavond ga ik uit.'

description: The many uses of 'er' in Dutch.

questions.question: In 'Er zijn drie stoelen,' what function does 'er' serve?
questions.explanation: 'Er zijn' is the existential construction meaning 'there are'.

questions.question: Rewrite with 'er': 'Ik praat over het probleem.' → 'Ik praat ___ over.'
questions.explanation: 'Er' replaces a prepositional phrase (over + het) → 'Ik praat er over' / 'Ik praat erover.'

questions.question: 'Er' as a location means roughly…
questions.explanation: 'Er' as a location pronoun refers back to a place just mentioned, like 'there'.

questions.question: Which is correct: 'I have three of them.'
questions.explanation: With quantities, 'er' precedes the number: 'Ik heb er drie (van).'

questions.question: Complete: 'Ik ben ___ blij mee.' (I am happy with it.)
questions.explanation: 'Ermee' / 'er … mee' replaces 'met het': 'Ik ben er blij mee.'

description: Learn the rules for forming small versions of nouns (-je, -tje, etc).

questions.question: What is the diminutive of 'boom' (tree)?
questions.explanation: Nouns ending in -m usually take -pje: boompje.

questions.question: What is the diminutive of 'tafel' (table)?
questions.explanation: Nouns ending in -l, -n, or -r after a long vowel take -tje: tafeltje.

questions.question: What is the diminutive of 'man'?
questions.explanation: Short vowel + n/m/l/r takes -etje: mannetje.

questions.question: What is the diminutive of 'huis'?
questions.explanation: Most nouns take -je: huisje.

questions.question: Which article is used for ALL diminutives?
questions.explanation: Diminutives are always 'het' words.

questions.question: What is the plural of 'boek'?
questions.explanation: Most Dutch nouns take -en: boeken.

questions.question: What is the plural of 'auto'?
questions.explanation: Nouns ending in a long vowel (except -e) take -'s: auto's.

questions.question: What is the plural of 'vader'?
questions.explanation: Nouns ending in -el, -em, -en, -er usually take -s: vaders.

questions.question: What is the plural of 'stad' (city)?
questions.explanation: 'Stad' is irregular: the plural is 'steden'.

questions.question: What happens to 'f' at the end of a noun in plural (e.g. 'brief')?
questions.explanation: F usually becomes V: brief → brieven.

description: Practice switching subject and verb when sentences don't start with the subject.

questions.question: Correct this: 'Vandaag ik werk.'
questions.explanation: If a sentence starts with something other than the subject, the verb comes first: Vandaag werk ik.

questions.question: When asking a question, where does the verb go?
questions.explanation: In yes/no questions, the verb starts the sentence: 'Werk jij vandaag?'

questions.question: What happens to the -t in 'jij werkt' during inversion?
questions.explanation: In inversion with 'jij' as the subject, the -t drops: 'Werk jij?'

questions.question: Does inversion happen after 'en', 'maar', or 'want'?
questions.explanation: Coordinating conjunctions (en, maar, want, of) do NOT cause inversion.

description: Common Dutch prepositions and their usage.

questions.question: Which preposition means 'to' (a city/country)?
questions.explanation: 'Naar' is used for directions: 'Ik ga naar Amsterdam'.

questions.question: Which preposition is used for 'waiting FOR'?
questions.explanation: 'Wachten op' is a fixed combination: 'Ik wacht op de bus'.

questions.question: Which preposition is used for 'thinking ABOUT'?
questions.explanation: 'Denken aan' is the standard way to say think about/of someone/something.

questions.question: Which preposition means 'at/by' (a person's house/business)?
questions.explanation: 'Bij' is used for 'at': 'Ik ben bij de kapper' (at the hairdresser).

description: Practice verbs where the action reflects back on the subject.

questions.question: How do you say 'I wash myself'?
questions.explanation: For 'ik', use the reflexive pronoun 'me' (or 'mij'): 'Ik was me'.

questions.question: What is the reflexive pronoun for 'u' (formal)?
questions.explanation: For 'u', use 'u' or 'zich': 'U wast u' or 'U wast zich'.

questions.question: What is the reflexive pronoun for 'hij/zij/het'?
questions.explanation: Singular and plural 3rd person (hij/zij/wij/jullie/zij) use 'zich': 'Hij wast zich'.

questions.question: Complete: 'Wij vergissen ___.' (We are mistaken.)
questions.explanation: For 'wij', the reflexive is 'ons': 'Wij vergissen ons'.

description: Learn how to tell time and talk about days in Dutch.

questions.question: How do you say 'half past two' in Dutch?
questions.explanation: Dutch says 'half' of the NEXT hour: 'half drie' is 2:30.

questions.question: What is 2:20 in Dutch?
questions.explanation: Between 20-40 mins, Dutch uses the half hour as a reference: 'tien voor half drie'.

questions.question: How do you say 'quarter to four'?
questions.explanation: 'Kwart voor vier' is 3:45.

questions.question: Which day is 'woensdag'?
questions.explanation: Maandag, dinsdag, WOENSDAG (Wednesday).
